bias: Coverage of the Texas GOP runoff starkly contrasts, with left-leaning outlets framing Senator Cornyn as "forced into" the contest, implying a setback, and using terms like "heated.
Right-Leaning perspectives portray it as a "GOP Battle," focusing on intra-party dynamics and providing specific vote percentages, while attributing the runoff to "Wesley Bryan.
Center-Leaning reports adopt a more neutral tone, describing the race as "competitive" or a "scramble," and uniquely offering a broader context by mentioning the Democratic field.

While all agree on the runoff outcome and DDHQ's projection, these distinct rhetorical tactics and emphases—from highlighting Republican vulnerability to internal party struggles—reveal underlying ideological priorities.
summary: On Tuesday, U.S. Sen. John Cornyn, R-Texas and Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton were projected to advance to a runoff after DecisionDesk showed Cornyn at about 43.2% and Paxton at 40.3%, with no candidate reaching 50%.
A crowded GOP field and Wesley Hunt's entry in October split votes, preventing any candidate from clearing 50%, leading to a May 26 runoff.

Big ad buys and outside groups intensified a GOP rift and drew national attention, with Cornyn and allied super PACs spending heavily to highlight his record in the costliest Senate primary ever, topping $125 million, while national parties watched closely.
The outcome in Texas feeds into a broader battle where Republicans must defend many seats while Democrats hunt pickups, with Cook rating the race as 'Likely Republican' and Kalshi giving 50% odds.
Early voting runs May 18 through May 22, with the winner facing Crockett or Talarico in the Nov. 3 general election.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the North Carolina Senate race as a "marquee" or "key" contest, employing language that elevates its significance and urgency.
Right-Leaning coverage, conversely, presents the candidates' advancement "amid little surprise," de-emphasizing drama.
Center-Leaning reports often share the "key" framing but also provide richer biographical context and highlight strategic implications, such as a "pickup opportunity" for Democrats.
A pivotal dividing line emerges in the explicit attribution of primary projections to specific news organizations by left and center sources, a detail the right de-emphasizes.
While all agree on the candidates and the November election, these differing portrayals of the race's importance reflect broader ideological approaches to political engagement.
summary: CNN projects Roy Cooper and Michael Whatley will advance to November for the North Carolina Senate seat, setting up a marquee contest to replace retiring Sen. Thom Tillis.

Amid registration changes, North Carolina hasn't elected a Democrat to the U.S. Senate since 2008, but strategists see this seat as a top pickup opportunity this year, with 39% unaffiliated voters shaping the race.
Campaigns differ: Cooper emphasizes affordability and Medicaid expansion, while Whatley focuses on crime and immigration, with Whatley raising nearly $6.5 million.

Democrats need to net four seats in November and will almost certainly need to flip Tillis's seat, while strategists say unaffiliated voters and national factors like the president's approval will decide this race.
Republicans point to Donald Trump's endorsement and Republican operatives say Whatley's expensive path will hinge on economy and president's standing as campaigns pivot to November.
title: Democrat Roy Cooper and Republican Michael Whatley set to face off in key North Carolina Senate race

bias: Left-Leaning outlets emphasize a "clash" between congressional Republicans and the Trump administration attempting to "downplay" ties, using terms like "embattled" and noting Lutnick "needs to answer for" his mentions in Hillary Clinton's deposition and specific 2005 neighbor timeline.

Right-Leaning coverage frames Lutnick as a "Top Trump Official" whose "Volunteers" to testify reveal the "Epstein Probe Widens," highlighting "disturbing elements" and a direct quote about "lunch with him on the island.
Center-Leaning reports echo the "clash" and "downplay" narratives, often calling Epstein a "disgraced financier.

All perspectives agree Lutnick will voluntarily testify regarding Epstein ties and claims innocence, revealing ideological divides over scrutinizing past administrations versus focusing on individual accountability within a broader scandal.
summary: U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick agreed to a transcribed interview with the House Oversight Committee, Axios and Reuters reported on Tuesday.
Justice Department Epstein files naming Lutnick triggered pressure, with Senate Democrats sending a records letter and House Democrats raising questions during recent testimony.
On last month, Lutnick told the Senate Appropriations Committee he visited Epstein's island with his family, but DOJ records show meetings in 2011 and 2012 that contrast with his denials.
Lutnick called Oversight Chair James Comer to volunteer, and White House spokesman Kush Desai praised his willingness to engage, with officials welcoming his cooperation.

The House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform will conduct a closed-door interview in the coming weeks, with a transcript reviewed by Lutnick's attorney, who states Lutnick has done nothing wrong.
title: Epstein files: Howard Lutnick volunteers to testify to House committee

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Trump's order as a reactive response to an "oil crisis" and "growing criticism," highlighting perceived inconsistencies and linking escalation to "U.S.-Israeli attacks against Iran .
They use terms like "threatens" and "war with Iran" with a negative tone.

Right-Leaning coverage, conversely, portrays the actions as decisive, a "bold escalation" to "protect" the economy and "ensure the free flow of energy," using commendatory language like "Unlocks" and "remarkable position.
Center-Leaning reports present Trump's statements directly, including the "if necessary" condition factually, and broaden the context with Iranian threats and potential "inflation" impacts.

While all acknowledge the goal to ensure "free flow of energy" through the vital Strait, the left emphasizes economic motives and skepticism, contrasting with the right's focus on strong leadership and national security, revealing ideological divides on executive power and conflict origins.
summary: On Tuesday, President Donald Trump announced the administration is considering deploying U.S. naval vessels to escort oil and gas tankers through the Strait of Hormuz and is meeting with Energy and Treasury secretaries to discuss options.
After U.S.-Israeli strikes over the weekend killed Iran’s supreme leader, U.S. crude prices surged nearly $10 per barrel since last week amid Iran targeting vessels in the Strait of Hormuz.

Examining government-backed maritime insurance is among the options under discussion, as marine insurance companies have sharply raised premiums for commercial tankers transiting the Strait of Hormuz.

The U.S. military reports it has sunk 11 Iranian ships since Saturday, shifting focus toward missile threats, and Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Monday the administration will unveil a plan Tuesday to address rising oil prices.
Qatar’s closure of a major export plant and attacks on Saudi Arabian refineries have tightened major LNG and oil shipments, risking further strain on U.S. stockpiles of air-defense interceptors.
title: Trump says US Navy will escort tankers through Strait of Hormuz 'if necessary'

summary: Colin Gray was found guilty of second-degree murder and all other charges related to his son's role in a mass shooting at Apalachee High School in Georgia.
The jury reached a verdict after just a few hours of deliberation following two weeks of testimony.
This case marks the first adult charged in connection with a school shooting in Georgia.
Prosecutors argued that Gray had sufficient warning of his son's dangerous behavior.
title: Jury finds father of accused Apalachee High School shooter guilty of murder, all other charges

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the outcome as a hard-right shift, stressing labels like "anti‑LGBTQ+" and "more extreme" to cast the win as a troubling purge, while right-leaning pieces celebrate " MAGA clout," praise the "purge" of disloyal figures and laud loyalty, using terms like "ousts" and "upset" to electrify readers; left uses critical, accusatory language, right uses triumphant, vindicating tones.
The decisive dividing lines are Trump endorsement/loyalty and Crenshaw’s record, which trigger starkly different moral narratives; center coverage is more procedural and de-emphasized in tone.
Both sides agree who won and that Trump alignment shaped the race.
summary: Republican challenger Steve Toth defeated U.S. Rep. Dan Crenshaw in the Texas 2nd Congressional District Republican primary race, according to CBS News projections.
Crenshaw, a former Navy SEAL who lost his right eye while serving in Afghanistan, was the only sitting House Republican in Texas not endorsed by former President Donald Trump.
Toth, a state representative and small business owner, argued that Crenshaw was not conservative enough and received the endorsement of Sen. Ted Cruz.
title: GOP challenger Steve Toth ousts Rep. Dan Crenshaw in Texas primary

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the US- Ecuador operation as either international cooperation or a "drastic expansion" of US military action, often scrutinizing the "narco-terrorism" label.

Right-Leaning coverage, however, employs terms like "Executes Military Operation" against "Narco-terrorists," emphasizing decisive action and linking it to "Trump's war" amidst "war rages on" rhetoric.
Center perspectives remain neutral, describing a "major expansion" without emotive language.
The designation of "terrorist organizations" and the "Trump administration's" role emerge as key ideological battlegrounds.

While all acknowledge joint efforts against drug trafficking and Ecuador's "explosion of violence," underlying ideological views on foreign intervention and national security shape these divergent portrayals.
summary: On Monday, Ecuador 's President Daniel Noboa announced the launch of joint operations with the United States and regional allies to fight drug trafficking in a `new phase`.
Ecuador's role as a transit hub helps explain the surge in violence, with around 70 percent of drugs from Colombia and Peru moving through the country and fueling deadly turf wars.

Officials said meetings in Quito involved senior US military leaders Francis Donovan, US Southern Command chief, and Mark Schafer, head of US Special Operations, while the United States deployed Air Force personnel to the former Manta base in December.
Noboa announced a curfew from March 15 to 30 in Guayas, Los Rios, Santo Domingo de los Tsachilas and El Oro, while Interior Minister John Reimberg urged residents to `Stay home.`

The moves position Ecuador within broader regional security efforts, as the joint operations aim to disrupt about 70 percent of regional cocaine routes, amid political sensitivity after a November referendum on foreign bases.
title: Ecuador Launches Joint Anti-Drug Operations with U.S.

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the AG's "demands" to resume "gender-affirming care" for "trans kids" as a righteous stand against "cowardice" and "capitulation" to the "Trump administration.

Right-Leaning outlets, however, portray the AG's "orders" for "gender-transition treatment for kids" with skepticism, using terms like "axes" for the hospital's prior action and de-emphasizing the political attribution of "funding threats from the federal government.
Center-Leaning reports remain neutral, focusing on the " Transgender Youth Health Program" and factual details like the "10 days" deadline.

While all agree the AG ordered the resumption of care, the stark divergence on the source of pressure and the characterization of youth medical services reveals deep ideological divides concerning state intervention and healthcare for minors.
summary: On March 3, 2026, the AG's office ordered NYU Langone to resume services for youths under 19 and gave a March 11 deadline for compliance.

Citing its recent departure of a medical director and the current regulatory environment, NYU Langone announced last month it would halt certain youth treatments, prompting the AG to order the hospital to resume services by March 11.

A Feb. 25 letter from the attorney general's office cited New York state anti-discrimination law, received multiple complaints, and said federal proposals do not change legal obligations, Darsana Srinivasan noted.
The AG's office said it would take 'further action' if NYU Langone does not resume care by March 11, but declined to specify steps; the hospital declined to comment.

Despite proposals not being final, the dispute highlights a clash between New York's anti-discrimination law and the Trump administration's funding threats, with Mount Sinai also pausing services in recent weeks.
title: NY attorney general demands hospital resume gender-affirming treatment for trans youth
topic_full: New York State Politics · New York

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames counties as "failing to use" Newsom's mental health court, emphasizing his "signature efforts" and "frustration" over implementation, particularly concerning "homelessness funds.
Conversely, right-leaning outlets portray Newsom as "accused of passing the buck" on "CA’s problems," linking his actions to "eyes White House" ambitions and suggesting he "foisted blame.
Center-Leaning reports neutrally on Newsom's "warns" to "improve implementation," providing specific program details and naming "10 counties" de-emphasized elsewhere.
While all acknowledge Newsom's funding threats for CARE Court, the fundamental divergence lies in framing it as a crucial social welfare initiative versus a politically driven maneuver.
summary: Newsom put 10 counties, including San Francisco and Los Angeles, on notice to improve their use of the state's CARE Court system to get homeless people struggling with severe mental illness into court-ordered treatment or risk losing funding.
Newsom said he is "happy to sweep all" if counties are "not producing the results" and noted that some counties like Napa and Merced are successfully using CARE Court.
The state will provide $291 million for supportive housing and behavioral health services statewide to strengthen enforcement of the CARE Act and accelerate housing and treatment expansion.
title: Newsom threatens California counties for failing to use his new mental health court

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the "deadly boat strikes" by emphasizing potential US culpability and human cost, aligning with scrutiny of foreign policy.
Right-Leaning coverage, while also using "deadly boat strikes," uniquely highlights the specific figure of "three people" killed, presenting it as a factual consequence without explicit blame.

Center-Leaning reports maintain neutrality, attributing statements directly with phrases like "prime minister says" and using "asserted" for claims, often de-emphasizing how the PM learned of the strike.

All perspectives agree on the Prime Minister's denial of authorization for the "alleged drug boat" strike, revealing a broader ideological divide between questioning authority and detailing immediate outcomes.
summary: On Tuesday, Prime Minister Godwin Friday said St. Vincent and the Grenadines did not authorize the Feb. 13 U.S. strike that killed three people and learned of it via social media.
The strikes began in early September and have killed at least 151 people as the administration of U.S. President Donald Trump targets those it calls `narcoterrorists` in small vessels.

Friday requested data on transit flows and questioned migrants' legal status while officials discussed using St. Vincent and other Caribbean nations as transit points for migrants intercepted at the U.S. southern border.
The incident immediately raised concerns as relatives said they believe Ricky Joseph, a 35-year-old boat captain from St. Lucia, was killed and people plying the waters want to know they are safe.

Friday said the aim is a coordinated approach within the Organization of Eastern Caribbean States, and the Caricom secretariat, a 15-member regional trade bloc, will coordinate humanitarian aid to Cuba.
title: St. Vincent did not give US authorization for deadly boat strikes, prime minister says
topic_full: Politics · Saint Vincent and the Grenadines

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the facility as a "detention camp" or "deportation camp," emphasizing a "spate" of prior outbreaks and its capacity of "more than 3,000 immigrants," suggesting systemic health issues and harsh conditions.
Right-Leaning coverage highlights the "outbreak" and "quarantine" with terms like "camp," subtly linking immigration to public health concerns.
Center-Leaning reports present factual information, identifying the lawmaker and distinguishing city from camp cases, noting "14 active measles cases" and "112 people isolated.
All perspectives agree on the camp's closure due to measles.
The contrasting narratives reveal ideological divides: the left focuses on detainee welfare and facility conditions, while the right emphasizes border security and public health risks.
summary: Tuesday, Camp East Montana in El Paso is under quarantine after a measles outbreak with 14 active cases and 112 people isolated until March 19 or 20.
Tuesday, Camp East Montana was placed under quarantine after reports of a measles outbreak, with 13 cases confirmed on Feb. 27 and conditions described as crowded and unsanitary by detainees.
DHS and ICE did not respond to repeated questions about contact tracing and outbreak handling, with journalists and translators barred from entry, and detainees only meeting counsel virtually.
U.S. Rep. Veronica Escobar called for the camp to be closed and for a DOJ probe, citing poor medical care after a $1.2 billion contract to Acquisition Logistics.

Despite limited state role, city of El Paso Department of Public Health confirmed 13 measles cases at Camp East Montana last week, with four in the community, and officials provided testing support and vaccines, Escobar warned.
title: A Large Immigration Detention Camp in Texas Is Closed to Visitors Amid Measles Outbreak

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes "thousands" of voters experiencing "confusion and frustration" due to new rules, portraying a systemic issue and highlighting Democratic calls for extended voting hours.

In stark contrast, right-leaning outlets, while acknowledging "confusion," pivot to discredit a Democratic figure, employing highly charged terms like "ELECTION DENIER!" and suggesting "Cheating" or "rigged" elections, de-emphasizing the scale of voter impact.

Center-Leaning reports, however, neutrally detail official responses, such as a judge's order to extend hours due to "voter confusion so severe" it crashed a website, and clarify the rule change from countywide voting.

While all perspectives agree voters were "turned away" by rule changes causing "confusion," the left focuses on voter access, the right on election integrity accusations, revealing deep ideological divides.
summary: On Tuesday, March 3, 2026, thousands of voters in Dallas County and Williamson County, Texas, showed up at wrong locations, prompting U.S. Rep. Jasmine Crockett to monitor and consider extending voting hours.

Local Republican county parties in Dallas and Williamson opted against countywide voting and implemented precinct-level, party-specific Election Day voting under state law requiring both parties' agreement, while recent redistricting and online voting-location listings also contributed to confusion.

At several Dallas sites, poll workers reported that voters were turned away at Lee McShan Elementary and Harry Stone Recreation Center, while former poll workers with tablets redirected many to correct precincts.

Judge Staci Williams approved an extension that keeps Democratic polling locations open until 9 p.m., with Dallas County Judge Clay Jenkins confirming provisional-ballot rules for votes cast from 7:00 p.m. to 9:00 p.m.
Campaigns immediately urged that voters check campaigns and county election websites, noting 73 early voting centers in Dallas County were open through Feb. 27.
title: Change in Primary Voting Rules Leads to Confusion in 2 Texas Counties as Voters Are Turned Away
At Least Four Killed in Israeli Strike on Building in Lebanon’s Baalbek

summary: On Monday, British Columbia Premier David Eby announced the province will adopt year-round Pacific daylight time, ending biannual clock changes after clocks move forward for a final time on Sunday.
Because some areas already use Mountain time, including Cranbrook, B.C., East Kootenay local governments are pushing to stay on Mountain time to remain in sync with Alberta year-round.
In Prince George, winter sunrises will be around 9:30 a.m., and Simon Yu advised `stock up on Vitamin D and set plenty of alarms.` Many hockey parents already wake at 5 a.m. in darkness.

Some residents welcomed a final decision, and Cranbrook Mayor Wayne Price said the council was surprised and wants talks `to see if a change is wanted here and to make sure our approach is co-ordinated.
title: B.C.’s move to end clock changes won’t be without complications

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes the victim's specific injuries and stable condition, portraying perpetrators neutrally as "young men" while de-emphasizing the attack's exact time.
Center-Leaning reports maintain a direct, factual tone, attributing information and focusing on police actions, but also de-emphasize victim's medical status.

In stark contrast, right-leaning outlets employ emotionally charged language, labeling perpetrators as a "Goon" and "ghoulish pals," consistently reporting three attackers, and de-emphasizing the victim's condition.
This reveals a divergence in focus from the victim's plight to the perceived depravity of the criminals.
Despite these narrative differences, all perspectives agree a 37-year-old man was set on fire near Penn Station while sleeping, and police are searching for suspects.
summary: On Monday, three men set a sleeping 37-year-old man on fire in Penn Station's Amtrak rotunda, and EMS extinguished the flames before rushing him to NewYork-Presbyterian/Weill Cornell burn unit.

Police say the motive remains unknown and is under investigation, amid scrutiny of Mayor Zohran Mamdani's Homeless Services Department after at least 19 New Yorkers died on the streets, 15 from hypothermia.

Police described the three suspects as one in a black jacket and blue jeans, another in a brown jacket, gray pants and gray hat, and the third in all black with a black backpack and shoulder-length hair.

NYPD detectives say no arrests have been made as suspects remain at large after fleeing into the Amtrak station; officials urged the public to call Crime Stoppers at 800-577-TIPS, 888-57-PISTA, or submit tips online and via X @NYPDTips.
Amtrak officials said Amtrak police are working with the NYPD to investigate the assault at Penn Station, noting a similar attack last year led to federal charges due to Amtrak's ownership.
title: NYPD Searches for Suspects After Homeless Man Set on Fire at Penn Station

bias: All perspectives agree on the core facts: two men arrested for a Cincinnati nightclub shooting that wounded nine, now facing federal charges.
However, center-leaning coverage distinctively frames the event as a "mass shooting" and emphasizes the "community looks to heal," introducing a more emotional, societal dimension.
In contrast, left-leaning reports add specific details like the arrest day and attribution, while right-leaning coverage remains most concise, de-emphasizing broader implications or emotional tones.

Despite these differences in emphasis and framing, all perspectives align on the involvement of the U.S. Attorney's Office and the number of wounded, revealing a shared commitment to reporting legal developments even as narrative priorities diverge.
summary: Authorities arrested two men Sunday in connection with a nightclub shooting that wounded nine, the Cincinnati Police Department said.
During a birthday celebration at Riverfront Live, a nightclub along the Ohio River, gunfire erupted around 1 a.m. Saturday, a witness reported hearing a fight before shots.

Officers responding to reports of gunfire found nine people with gunshot wounds and took them to hospitals where officials say they are in stable condition, while the department's homicide unit investigates with FBI assistance and the ATF previously offered a $5,000 reward.
Management representing Jermaine Tandy said they were `devastated` and called it a `senseless act`, and no attorneys for the two were yet listed in court records as of Tuesday morning.
The venue sits along the Ohio River near Riverbend Music Center and has a troubled recent history, including criminal activity, before rebranding in 2018.
title: 2 men arrested in Cincinnati nightclub shooting are facing federal charges

bias: All perspectives report a prosecutor's allegations that the Alexander brothers used "wealth" to "lure, drug and rape" women and girls, and bragged in blog posts.

Left-Leaning coverage frames this with an accusatory tone, emphasizing "wealth" as an exploitative tool and highlighting "women and girls" as vulnerable victims, uniquely reporting "alleging defense intimidated victims.
Right-Leaning headlines present the allegations more directly, de-emphasizing broader social critiques.
Center-Leaning outlets focus on attributing allegations to the "prosecutor says" and detailing procedural aspects like "four weeks" of testimony and the "not guilty" plea.
These approaches reveal differing priorities in covering the same core legal proceedings.
summary: Earlier this week, the defense for Tal, Alon, and Oren Alexander began their case in New York City federal court after prosecutors alleged witness intimidation.
Prosecutors say the brothers repeatedly recruited women, drugged and raped them across Hamptons, New York City, and other locations over more than a decade, with over 60 accusations since 2024.

Digital evidence—videos, photos and messages—formed a cornerstone of the prosecution's presentation, including a video of alleged assault and a photo of a sleeping topless girl found on brothers' devices, Andrew Jones said.

Prosecutors rested earlier than planned, foreshadowing dropped charges tied to absent witnesses; the jury of six women and six men plus one alternate will hear closing arguments likely later this week.

Jacqueline Perczek said the defense will use photo metadata and private staff testimony to show inconsistencies and support alibis, and Agnifilo said one or more brothers may testify, with the defense able to rest as soon as Tuesday.
title: Alexander Brothers Used Wealth to Lure, Drug and Rape Women and Girls, Prosecutor Says

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the Epstein settlement within a broader political narrative, employing emotionally charged language like "brutal reality check" and "cornered like a rat" to portray a Republican's warning about President Trump's potential "attacking Iran" as a distraction from the "Epstein files.

The "$35 million settlement" is presented by right-leaning outlets as a straightforward legal outcome, a perspective shared by center coverage which also details the lawsuit and notes the political courage of a "Rare Republican Who Is Not Afraid of Trump.
A pivotal dividing line is the left's emphasis on an alleged "Operation Epic Fury" and its implications, a topic entirely de-emphasized elsewhere.

While all perspectives acknowledge the preliminary approval of the settlement, this stark contrast reveals ideological priorities: political accountability and motive scrutiny versus legal process and de-emphasis of partisan conflict.
summary: Jeffrey Epstein predicted that if Donald Trump felt cornered, he would start a war with Iran to rally public support, as indicated in the Epstein files.
Republican Representative Thomas Massie criticized the strikes as unauthorized acts of war and said bombing Iran would not distract from the Epstein files controversy.
Critics, including former Representative Marjorie Taylor Greene, urged focusing on transparency and justice related to the Epstein files rather than the foreign military actions.
title: $35M settlement between Epstein estate, accusers wins preliminary approval

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes RFK Jr.'s "politicization of research" as the impetus for a new autism panel, employing a critical tone.

Right-Leaning accounts de-emphasize this specific motivation, framing the group as a "counter to Kennedy-appointed group" and a "science-based alternative," often highlighting opposition to a politicized figure.

Center-Leaning reports bridge these, citing "potential vaccine misinformation" and RFK Jr. "reshaping" the federal committee, using terms like "rebuke Kennedy" while also presenting the "science-based alternative.
All perspectives agree an independent group formed as a "counter" to a Kennedy-affiliated entity, revealing ideological divides over scientific authority and political influence.
summary: On Tuesday, autism researchers and advocates announced the Independent Autism Coordinating Committee to develop a scientific agenda and hold its first meeting on March 19 alongside the Interagency Autism Coordinating Committee.
Facing the federal overhaul, researchers organized an independent alternative after Kennedy appointed 21 new IACC members, some supporting vaccine–autism claims, as said Tager‑Flusberg.
The 12-member Independent ACC, mostly researchers including Joshua Gordon, MD, PhD, and Alison Singer, plans same-day meetings on vaccines and contested topics mirroring the federal schedule.
HHS said it will not alter the federal panel's proceedings, while Alison Singer said the effort is needed to ensure `science, not misinformation, guides autism research`.

With about 2 billion in annual autism research funding at stake, the Independent ACC says it will fulfill the Autism CARES Act objective, Helen Tager‑Flusberg, PhD, said, while its first meeting agenda targets non‑evidence based treatments.
title: Autism researchers form independent committee as counter to Kennedy-appointed group

bias: Left-Leaning narratives frame Democrats as steadfast, "won't relent" on "demands" against an "Iran war," emphasizing their firm stance.

Right-Leaning coverage, conversely, portrays Democrats as playing "DHS Funding Games" that are "Indefensible" after a "TX Terror Attack," even accusing them of "Abetting Terrorists" with highly charged language.
Center-Leaning reports, while noting Democrats "Won't Budge," generally present actions like "Jeffries rejects" more neutrally, uniquely detailing Democratic demands to "rein in ICE and CBP.
All perspectives agree Republicans are pushing for DHS funding amid "fighting intensifies overseas," but the right exclusively cites domestic "terror threats.
This reveals a core ideological split between national security urgency and federal agency oversight.
summary: Amid a partial DHS shutdown, Senate Democrats refuse to reopen full funding until immigration -enforcement reforms are secured, leaving DHS agencies operating without pay.

Negotiations between Congress and the White House have centered on immigration enforcement changes since DHS funding expired on Feb. 13, prompting a partial shutdown, with all parties demanding reforms.
Legislative constraints matter: passing a DHS bill needs 60 Senate votes, yet Republicans hold 53 seats, and House GOP leaders plan a Thursday vote on a modified DHS funding bill.
Tennessee Republicans warned that a funding lapse could hinder efforts to identify sleeper cells, with six American service members killed and U.S. embassies attacked since U.S. strikes on Iran.
Sen. Jon Ossoff argued Congress can fund DHS while imposing basic standards on immigration agents, reaffirming accountability demands, and still ensure agencies protecting Americans are funded.
title: Jeffries rejects Republican bid to fund DHS amid Iran conflict

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the Smoothie King incident around employee perspectives, highlighting claims of harassment from the "right-wing" and dismissing " MAGA cries 'discrimination'" as disingenuous.
It emphasizes the "Black employees" and their GoFundMe, de-emphasizing the couple's legal actions.

Right-Leaning outlets portray the event as "discrimination" against Trump supporters, using terms like "Liberal College Town" and "leftist demonization" to underscore a broader narrative of conservative victimhood, detailing the couple's police report and lawyer.
Center-Leaning reports maintain a neutral tone, focusing on company responses.
While all agree on the employees' termination, the stark contrast reveals ideological divides concerning political expression, perceived discrimination, and online accountability.
summary: Following its investigation, Smoothie King announced on X that the franchise owner took immediate action and the two employees involved are no longer with the business .
After the incident in Ann Arbor on Sunday, March 1, Erika Lindemyer posted a TikTok showing employees refused service because her husband wore a pro‑Trump hoodie, citing discomfort.
The clip had more than 276,000 views by noon Monday and was reshared widely across X and TikTok; Williams started a GoFundMe that raised $415 as of 2 PM Monday.
Both the franchise owner and Smoothie King reached out to apologize and the local owner ordered mandatory retraining for all employees to reinforce guest-experience standards.
Social reactions were split, with some users supporting the employees and others backing the company's decision, reflecting broader political polarization during Trump's second term.
title: Smoothie King: 2 employees ‘no longer with the business’ after refusing to serve customer in Trump shirt

bias: News coverage of a drone strike on a CIA station reveals stark contrasts.
Left-Leaning outlets frame the event as Iranian "retaliation" for a "U.S.-Israeli bombing campaign," noting "no indication the station was the target," thus de-emphasizing direct intent.

Conversely, right-leaning outlets employ alarmist language like "obliterated" and characterize it as a "major win for Iran," portraying a significant defeat and linking it to a broader pattern of attacks.
Center coverage cautiously reports "minor damage" and "persistent Middle East tensions," highlighting an embassy warning.

While all agree on a suspected Iranian drone hitting the station, the differing portrayals of intent and impact—from accidental collateral to a strategic "win"—underscore deep ideological divides regarding foreign policy and regional power dynamics.
summary: On Monday, the Central Intelligence Agency's station inside the U.S. Embassy in Riyadh was struck by two suspected Iranian drones, with a March 3 photo showing damage after the attack.
Amid escalating regional strikes following U.S. and Israeli operations, Iran has retaliated with drone and missile strikes targeting Israel and Gulf countries hosting U.S. military assets.
An internal State Department alert said the attack collapsed part of the embassy roof and filled interiors with smoke, while Embassy personnel sheltered in place with no CIA casualties.
Washington's mission in Saudi Arabia warned Americans to avoid the embassy and canceled routine and emergency U.S. citizen services appointments on Tuesday, raising regional security concerns.
There is no indication the CIA station was the target, and the embassy is among U.S. sites hit in the Middle East escalation following Saturday's operations.
title: CIA station in Saudi Arabia struck by suspected Iranian drone, source says

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the tragedy with terms like "violent," portraying the community as "stunned," and de-emphasizing socio-economic context.
Right-Leaning coverage highlights the "affluent Florida community" and names the mother, while de-emphasizing the father's role in the welfare check.
Center-Leaning reports blend factual details, such as the "$1.4 million home" value and "exclusive" neighborhood, with emotionally charged terms like "slaughter" and "stupefied.

While all perspectives acknowledge the official murder-suicide investigation and use "violent/violently," the left focuses on the act's brutality, whereas the right and center emphasize the unexpected nature of such an event in a prosperous setting.
These distinct portrayals underscore differing ideological lenses on tragedy, from its immediate human impact to its broader societal context.
summary: On Thursday night, Manatee County Sheriff's Office deputies responded to the 8200 block of Pavia Way and found a woman and two children dead inside a Lakewood Ranch home.
After a call from a homeowner overseas requesting a welfare check, deputies were sent because the concerned father could not reach his family, who moved from Missouri about three years ago.

Deputies said inside they found a 14-year-old boy, an 11-year-old girl and their 44-year-old mother dead in separate rooms, and investigators say they are confident this was a murder-suicide with no one else entering or leaving.

Investigators say the probe is active and detectives are processing the scene and reviewing evidence, while The Lake Club at Lakewood Ranch HOA acknowledged the tragedy and neighbors in the Lake Club community expressed shock.
title: Mother in affluent Florida community killed 2 children before taking her own life: police

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the incident as an "Iran war," emphasizing its initiation by "President Donald Trump launched" and detailing broader regional retaliation, including "18 others were wounded.
Right-Leaning coverage attributes direct blame via "Iranian drone attack," employing terms like "Operation Epic Fury" and humanizing soldiers as "devoted parents.
Center coverage, while also noting a "deadly Iranian drone strike" and "emotional tributes," de-emphasizes political framing, offering personal military history.

All perspectives agree on the four identified Army Reserve members killed in Kuwait by a drone strike, yet diverge significantly on the conflict's nature and responsibility, reflecting underlying ideological stances on military intervention and foreign policy.
summary: Pentagon officials on Tuesday named four American soldiers killed on March 1 at Port Shuaiba, Kuwait after a drone strike hit a command center.
In the opening hours of the campaign, four U.S. service members died after the Feb. 28 strikes that targeted Iran's leadership, according to the Pentagon.
The Department of Defense named 1st Class Nicole M. Amor, 39, and Class Noah L. Tietjens, 42, with the 103rd Sustainment Command in Des Moines, Iowa.

U.S. military investigators said the attack is under investigation, Central Command reported at least five troops were seriously wounded, and President Donald Trump was expected to brief Congress Tuesday.
Local communities and Iowa leaders mourned Sgt. Declan J. Coady, 20, West Des Moines native and Drake University student, after his death in the Iran conflict on Tuesday.
title: Pentagon releases names of 4 of 6 troops killed in Iran war and says they died in a drone strike in Kuwait
Periods, Old Age and Communal Conflict: Oscar Shorts ...

bias: Left-Leaning coverage "decries" the U.S.-Israeli missile attack as "arrogant," employing satirical language like "smarter than our president" and questioning the cost of "start an entire war.

Conversely, right-leaning outlets frame the action as a necessary "mission to destroy" "Iran missile, naval threats," using decisive terms like "attack" and highlighting Secretary Rubio's statements on military objectives while de-emphasizing regime change.
Center-Leaning reports neutrally present Rubio's comments on mitigating "oil price spikes" and the mission's scope.

While all acknowledge the strikes and the death of Iran's Supreme Leader, these narratives underscore fundamental ideological divides concerning intervention, leadership, and national security priorities.
summary: On Monday, Secretary of State Marco Rubio told reporters on Capitol Hill that Operation Epic Fury aims to eliminate Iran's short-range ballistic missiles and naval capabilities, saying `That is the clear objective of this mission`.
On February 28, coordinated U.S. and Israeli strikes began that targeted multiple Iranian sites as officials expected retaliation and sought to reduce U.S. casualties, Rubio said.

Reports from Iranian and international sources indicate six U.S. service members have died, 555 people were killed across Iran, and Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and four senior officials were killed in strikes.

Iran launched retaliatory strikes that targeted U.S. assets in the Gulf and Israel while Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait and Jordan also faced attacks, raising concern among Democrats, critics and some Republicans about the unclear mission timeline.
Rubio said the mission's goal is destruction of Iran's missile and naval capabilities, disputing that it is about regime change, according to the source.
title: Rubio says U.S. mission in Iran targets missile, naval threats

summary: On Monday, Israeli settlers killed two brothers in Qaryout, south of Nablus, the Palestinian Red Crescent reported; three others were injured by gunfire.

Since the Gaza war began, violence in the West Bank has surged, with the Wall and Settlement Resistance Commission recording around 4,723 cases of settler violence during 2025, and recent killings add to this trend.
Settlers fired live rounds and burned properties during the assault, while crowds tried to rescue victims and ambulances faced delays amid tear gas fired by Israeli forces.
Wounded were taken to Nablus hospital as Israel's military said it was `looking into` the reports, including a 15-year-old boy and three others.

The U.N. humanitarian office reported last year the highest daily average of settler attacks since 2006, while Palestinian residents and rights groups say Israeli authorities routinely fail to prosecute settlers, worsening impunity.
title: Israeli Settler Gunfire Kills Two Palestinian Brothers in West Bank

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the missile strike and spy cell bust by highlighting "no human loss" and "escalating regional tensions," even mentioning Israeli actions on the Lebanese border.

Conversely, right-leaning outlets amplify the incident's gravity, using urgent terms like "DEVELOPING" and "BREAKING," emphasizing the "Largest US Military Base" was "Hit" by a "Ballistic Missile," and sensationalizing the spy arrests as "In a First" with "10" individuals.
Center perspectives offer a more concise, attributed account, describing the broader situation as "Mideast war" without the left's specific casualty details or the right's alarmist framing.

While all acknowledge the missile strike and spy cell bust, the left focuses on de-escalation and broader regional impacts, while the right underscores foreign aggression and national security threats, reflecting fundamental ideological divides on conflict interpretation.
summary: On Wednesday, March 04, 2026, Israel's military launched a broad wave of strikes on targets in Iran, shortly after midnight a loud explosion was heard in Beirut, AFP journalist said.

After multiple missile barrages, Israel responded with strikes; Iran launched three missile barrages at Israeli territory, and Hezbollah fired rockets early Monday in retaliation for the reported killing of Ali Khamenei.
Admiral Brad Cooper, US commander in the Middle East , said `nearly 2,000 targets` were struck in Iran, severely degrading air defenses and destroying ballistic missiles, launchers and drones.

The United States arranged charter flights from Jordan, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates to help Americans leave and will secure additional capacity as security allows, while France is sending the Charles de Gaulle aircraft carrier to the eastern Mediterranean.

Qatar's State Security Service said they dismantled two spy cells linked to Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, arresting 10 suspects who admitted espionage and sabotage roles, seizing communications equipment, data and coordinates.
title: Iranian Missile Strikes Biggest U.S. Air Base in the Middle East, Qatar Says

bias: Left-Leaning outlets emphasize the human cost, highlighting "cluster missile wounds" and framing the target as a "group behind electing" Iran's leader, often using passive voice like "Came Under Fire.

This contrasts sharply with right-leaning coverage, which employs aggressive terms such as "Bombs" and "Flattens" to portray a decisive military action aimed at "disrupting the regime’s ability" to replace Khamenei, even suggesting an intent to "kill all the members.

While center-leaning reports provide neutral context on the "Assembly of Experts," the left focuses on "12 wounded" and broader conflict, whereas the right touts a "dead halt, literally" to the succession.

All perspectives agree on an Israeli strike targeting the body responsible for electing Iran's Supreme Leader post-Khamenei, but diverge on the strike's legitimacy and impact, reflecting ideological splits on military intervention and humanitarian concerns versus strategic disruption.
summary: On March 3, 2026, Israel 's military struck the Assembly of Experts' Qom office while it was meeting to select a successor, severely damaging the building, security officials said.
Because the Assembly chooses the next supreme leader, its meeting followed Sunday’s confirmation of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's death and plans for an interim leadership council.

Escalation on the conflict's fourth day saw Israeli ground troops deployed into Lebanon and explosions in Tehran, while Iran struck the U.S. embassy in Saudi Arabia as Washington withdrew staff, with hundreds killed mostly in Iran.
Analysts warn the spiralling conflict raises questions about how long and how it will end, while U.S. President Donald Trump said it could last four to five weeks but the US is prepared to go longer.
title: Israel says it hit Iran’s Assembly of Experts as it was meeting to choose Khamenei’s successor

summary: Following recent retaliatory strikes, the Prime Minister allowed the US to use British bases including Diego Garcia, Chagos Islands, and RAF Fairford, Gloucestershire, for defensive actions on Sunday.
Because Iran's retaliatory strikes threatened British people and allies, the UK changed stance to protect over 300,000 British citizens in countries targeted by Iran.
Senior US statements outlined aims to degrade Iran's missile capabilities, with Trump publicly rebuking Sir Keir for initially refusing permission as US and Israeli forces struck Iran on Saturday.
Hours after the permission, a drone struck RAF Akrotiri in Cyprus; Defence Secretary John Healey said damage was minimal and families of service personnel were moved away as a precaution.

Ministers argued the move was taken on legal and national-interest grounds, insisting decisions rely on `legality` and British interests, while Conservative MPs accused Sir Keir of undermining the `special relationship`.
title: UK Still Working Closely with US, Minister Insists, After Trump Criticism

bias: Reports consistently indicate the Trump administration is exploring support for armed groups to destabilize Iran , with the CIA reportedly arming Kurdish forces to foment an uprising.

Left-Leaning coverage frames this as a "Regime Change War," using terms like "secretly arming" and "flirting with armed militias," emphasizing historical "anti-American sentiment" and potential "US boots on the ground.

Right-Leaning outlets portray it as a strategic effort to "topple weakened regime" or "Defeat Iranian Regime," employing assertive language like "Ignite Uprising" and predicting an imminent "ground campaign" to minimize direct US involvement.
Center-Leaning reports maintain a neutral tone, often questioning the clarity of objectives.
These contrasting narratives reveal fundamental ideological divides on foreign intervention and its consequences.
summary: Recent reporting indicates the CIA is coordinating with Iraqi Kurdistan to arm Iranian Kurdish forces for a potential uprising, CNN reports.
Outreach by the administration included a recent presidential call that aimed to pin down Iranian security forces so unarmed Iranian civilians can rise without mass reprisals like January's unrest.

Near the Iraq–Iran border, Kurdish armed groups numbering in the thousands maintain a strong presence as Israeli military strikes western Iran and Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps target Kurdish forces with drones.
U.S. intelligence cautions that the Kurds lack what it would take to succeed, noting failure could reinforce claims of U.S. abandonment and undermine Iraqi sovereignty.

Analysts point out past CIA-Kurdish links and broader limits of external regime-change efforts, noting the CIA's 1953 covert operations in Iran caused deep blowback and Iraqi Kurdish factions host a CIA outpost.
title: CIA working to arm Kurdish forces to spark uprising in Iran, sources say

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the testimony of a Goldman Sachs lawyer as a "grilling" for an "Ex-White House lawyer" concerning "luxury gifts" from Epstein, emphasizing intense scrutiny and potential misconduct.
Right-Leaning outlets concisely focus on a "Goldman Sachs lawyer" facing Congress over "Epstein ties," portraying elite accountability.
Center perspectives neutrally report the request to testify, explicitly linking her departure to "fallout over her emails with Jeffrey Epstein," a detail de-emphasized by other coverage.

While all acknowledge the testimony regarding Epstein and her departure, the left employs emotionally charged terms like "grilling," contrasting with the right's more direct portrayal of elite scrutiny.
These divergent portrayals underscore ideological differences in how powerful figures and their associations are scrutinized.
summary: On Tuesday, the House Oversight Committee summoned Kathryn Ruemmler, departing Goldman Sachs general counsel, to testify about her apparent close relationship with Jeffrey Epstein, believing she has key information.

Amid batches of released documents, Congress and the Justice Department deepened Goldman Sachs' concerns about Kathryn Ruemmler, who announced her resignation last month and will leave at the end of June nearly three weeks later.
Records released by investigators reveal Epstein's documents listed Ruemmler as a backup executor, called her the night of his 2019 arrest, and detailed chatty emails and luxury gifts, Connelly said.

Jennifer Connelly, Ruemmler's spokeswoman, said she welcomes the opportunity to appear before the Oversight Committee and maintained Ruemmler had done nothing wrong, while Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick agreed to testify earlier Tuesday.

These developments position the probe to influence Goldman Sachs' reputation and leadership, as the House Oversight Committee has been holding hearings including interviewing Bill and Hillary Clinton last week.
title: House Committee Wants Goldman Sachs General Counsel to Testify on Epstein

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the DOJ's reversal as "Trump's battle" and a "whiplash-inducing move," emphasizing "punitive" and "unconstitutional" orders with critical, sarcastic tones.
Right-Leaning coverage employs terms like "Stunning Flip-Flop" and "retaliation" against "Dem-connected" firms, suggesting erratic, partisan actions.
Center coverage neutrally reports the "reversal" and uniquely attributes it to Trump's "ire," a detail de-emphasized by the right, which states no explanation was offered.

While all perspectives agree the DOJ reversed course within 24 hours to continue defending executive orders, the left highlights prior judicial rulings against them, a detail largely de-emphasized by the right.
These portrayals underscore ideological divides over governmental accountability and perceived political motivation.
summary: On March 3, 2026, the DOJ filed to withdraw its request to dismiss appeals in the DC Circuit, reversing a Monday decision to drop its defense of President Trump's executive orders.
With no public explanation from the department, reporting tied the about-face to White House Counsel's Office discussions and backlash from Trump and top aide Stephen Miller.

The four firms — Perkins Coie, WilmerHale, Jenner & Block and Susman Godfrey — won at the trial level, with four federal district court judges finding the executive orders unconstitutional; the directives sought to suspend security clearances, restrict federal access and review contracts.
Facing a procedural scramble, the four firms oppose the `unexplained about-face` after an email around 10 a.m. gave them about a half hour to oppose, and the three-judge panel has not yet ruled.
Beyond the immediate fight, nine other firms struck deals pledging roughly $1 billion in pro bono work to avoid similar orders, and Paul, Weiss agreed to provide $40 million in such services.
title: DOJ reverses on ending fight over Trump orders targeting law firms

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames US actions as aggressively "turning up heat" and a "threat to indict" Venezuela's interim leader, emphasizing coercion.

Right-Leaning outlets, conversely, portray the "Trump Admin" as assertively "Threatening" a "Maduro Successor's Future," often using rhetorical questions like "Delcy Indictment ? Machado's Homecoming?" to imply desired outcomes and broader political shifts.

A pivotal dividing line emerges with right-leaning sources reporting an official denial of the indictment, a detail de-emphasized by left and center perspectives, with center coverage framing the action as a strategic "leverage" tool.

While all sides acknowledge US involvement concerning Delcy Rodriguez and the legal case, their differing interpretations of US intent—from aggressive pressure to justified assertion—reveal underlying ideological views on foreign policy and regime change.
summary: On March 3, Reuters reported that the U.S. Attorney's Office in Miami is preparing a draft indictment against Delcy Rodriguez as part of a U.S. effort to strengthen leverage with Caracas, while the Department of Justice declined to comment.
After the Jan. 3 capture of Maduro, Rodriguez took power roughly two months later, with Trump praising her for stabilizing Venezuela and securing oil access.
The probe targets alleged PDVSA-linked laundering from 2021 to 2025, with Interior Minister Diosdado Cabello and Defense Minister Vladimir Padrino already indicted, while grand juries meet in secret.

U.S. officials handed Rodriguez a list seeking at least seven individuals for arrest or potential extradition, delivered by Laura Dogu, U.S. envoy to Venezuela, but Venezuelan law on extradition prohibits nationals' transfer.
Alex Saab's case illustrates the stakes: he was arrested in early February, remains detained by SEBIN, and faces a sealed money‑laundering indictment with unclear extradition prospects.
title: Justice Department Reportedly Preparing Criminal Indictment Against Venezuela's Interim Leader Delcy Rodriguez

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the billionaire wealth tax as an investment in "working families" providing "$3,000 checks," emphasizing "fair share" and an " economy that works for all of us.

In stark contrast, right-leaning coverage employs alarmist language like "Goes Nuclear" and "Craziest Tax Proposal Yet," asserting the tax would "make all Americans poorer" and is a "Tax Increase" that will "impoverish Americans.

While center outlets neutrally report the "$4.4 trillion" proposal, the left highlights direct benefits, and the right dismisses the figure's viability, portraying proponents as a "socialist of Vermont.
The core disagreement lies in whether taxing the wealthy benefits or harms the broader economy, revealing deep ideological splits on wealth redistribution and government's role.
summary: On Monday, Sen. Bernie Sanders and Rep. Ro Khanna introduced the Make Billionaires Pay Their Fair Share Act, proposing a 5% annual wealth tax on 938 billionaires worth $8.2 trillion.
Amid widening inequality, sponsors say 938 billionaires grew $1.5 trillion richer while UC Berkeley economists Saez and Zucman project $4.4 trillion in revenue over the next decade.
The proposal would allocate revenue to fund $3,000 direct payments per eligible person while Elon Musk would owe $42 billion, and Jeff Bezos and Mark Zuckerberg about $11 billion each.
Because Congress opposes the bill, Khanna and Sanders face donor backlash and opposition from Gov. Gavin Newsom, with some state billionaire tax efforts prompting relocations.

Because it taxes net worth, the law could force asset sales as billionaires without liquid assets might sell holdings to pay bills, while critics of revenue projections cite behavioral responses and avoidance.
title: Sanders proposes $4.4 trillion tax on billionaires
